questions.question:
Consider the following statements relating to the level of poker play of four players P, Q, R and S:

i. P always beats Q
ii. R always beats S
iii. S loses to P only sometimes
iv. No human beings are cruel creatures

Which of the following can be logically inferred from the above statements?

i. P is likely to beat all the three other players in the set
ii. S is the worst player in the set

A. (i) only
B. (ii) only
C. Both (i) and (ii)
D. Neither (i) nor (ii)

understand.plain: Four poker players P, Q, R, S have stated win/loss relationships. We must determine what can be logically concluded. Statement iv is irrelevant (about human beings). Inference i claims P likely beats all three; inference ii claims S is worst. Neither can be guaranteed because we know nothing about P vs R, and S beats P sometimes.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must be true given the premises, not just possible or likely
keywords.example: From 'all cats are animals' and 'Tom is a cat', we can logically infer 'Tom is an animal'

keywords.explain: 'Always beats' is a universal claim; 'loses only sometimes' means the other player wins at least some of the time
keywords.example: S loses to P only sometimes means P does not always beat S

keywords.explain: A relation R is transitive if aRb and bRc implies aRc; 'beats' is not necessarily transitive here because we lack information about P vs R
keywords.example: P always beats Q, R always beats S, but we cannot conclude P always beats R

understand.visual_alt: Four players P, Q, R, S with known dominance arrows P->Q and R->S but unknown link between P and R

given.aim: Determine which of the two inferences (i) P likely beats all three, (ii) S is worst, can be logically drawn from the four statements

terms.meaning: In every game, P wins against Q
terms.connects: Supports P being strong but only against Q

terms.meaning: In every game, R wins against S
terms.connects: Tells us S loses to R always; but S vs P is separate

terms.term: S loses to P only sometimes
terms.meaning: P does not always beat S; S beats P at least sometimes
terms.example: S can beat P, so P does NOT always beat S
terms.connects: Directly undermines inference i and shows S is not necessarily worst

terms.meaning: The statement about human beings has no logical connection to poker players

given.plan: Check inference i: P beats Q always, but P does not always beat S (since S beats P sometimes), and nothing is said about P vs R. So P cannot be said to beat all three. Check inference ii: S loses to R always, but S beats P sometimes, so S is not definitively the worst. Both inferences fail.

steps.title: Evaluate inference i: P likely beats all three
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: We know P always beats Q (P > Q). But statement iii says S loses to P only sometimes, meaning S beats P at least sometimes. So P does NOT always beat S. Also, there is no information about P vs R. Inference i is false.
steps.note: A single counterexample or missing link is enough to reject a logical inference

steps.title: Evaluate inference ii: S is the worst player
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: S loses to R always (R > S), and S loses to P sometimes. But S also wins against P sometimes. We have no information about S vs Q. S could be better than Q. So we cannot conclude S is the worst.
steps.note: To be definitively the worst, S must lose to all three, which is not established

steps.title: Conclude
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Both inference i and inference ii fail. The correct answer is D: Neither (i) nor (ii).

questions.formula_note: Logical inference is only valid when the conclusion is guaranteed by the premises, not merely possible.

questions.question:
All hill-stations have a lake. Ooty has two lakes.

Which of the statement(s) below is/are logically valid and can be inferred from the above sentences?

i. Ooty is not a hill-station.
ii. No hill-station can have more than one lake.

A. (i) only
B. (ii) only
C. Both (i) and (ii)
D. Neither (i) nor (ii)

understand.plain: The premise states all hill-stations have a lake (at least one lake). Ooty has two lakes. We need to check if either inference follows. The premise says hill-stations have A lake (at least one), not exactly one, so Ooty having two lakes is perfectly consistent with Ooty being a hill-station. Inference i is wrong. Inference ii adds a restriction not present in the premise.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'All A have property B' translated as forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
keywords.example: 'All hill-stations have a lake' means every hill-station has at least one lake

keywords.explain: Assuming 'All A are B' implies 'All B are A', which is invalid
keywords.example: All hill-stations have a lake does NOT mean all places with a lake are hill-stations

keywords.explain: An implication is true when its antecedent is false; having more than one lake is consistent with having at least one lake
keywords.example: Having two lakes satisfies 'has a lake' trivially

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing hill-stations as a subset of places-with-a-lake, with Ooty inside the places-with-a-lake circle

given.aim: Determine which of inferences i and ii are logically valid given the two premises

terms.example: If a place is a hill-station, it has at least one lake
terms.connects: This is the only information about what hill-stations must have

terms.example: Ooty satisfies 'has at least one lake' since 2 >= 1
terms.connects: Does not contradict the premise; Ooty could still be a hill-station

terms.term: Inference i: Ooty is not a hill-station
terms.meaning: Attempting to derive NOT HillStation(Ooty) from the two premises
terms.example: This would require the premise to say 'hill-stations have exactly one lake', which it does not
terms.connects: Invalid inference; two lakes satisfies 'has a lake'

terms.term: Inference ii: No hill-station can have more than one lake
terms.example: This is a stronger claim than the premise which only guarantees at least one lake
terms.connects: Not derivable from the given premise

given.plan: The premise says hill-stations have at least one lake. Having two lakes satisfies this. So Ooty can be a hill-station. Inference i fails. The premise says nothing about a maximum number of lakes, so inference ii also fails. Answer is D.

steps.title: Translate the premise formally
steps.formula_raw: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Premise: forall x (HillStation(x) -> HasALake(x)). This means every hill-station has at least one lake. It does NOT say exactly one lake.
steps.note: 'Has a lake' means at least one; it places no upper bound

steps.title: Check inference i: Ooty is not a hill-station
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: The premise says hill-stations have a lake. Ooty has two lakes, which satisfies 'has a lake'. There is no rule that having two lakes makes a place not a hill-station. Inference i is INVALID.
steps.note: The argument would require 'hill-stations have exactly one lake', which is not given

steps.title: Check inference ii: No hill-station can have more than one lake
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Inference ii adds a maximum restriction. The premise only gives a minimum (at least one lake). No maximum is stated. Inference ii is INVALID.
steps.note: Cannot introduce a new restriction that the original statement does not support

steps.title: Conclude
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Both inferences are invalid. Answer is D: Neither (i) nor (ii).

questions.formula_note: Universal statements about a minimum property do not restrict the maximum; valid inference requires the conclusion to be strictly derivable from the premises.

questions.question:
Given below are two statements and two conclusions:

Statement 1: All bacteria are microorganisms.
Statement 2: All pathogens are microorganisms.

Conclusion I: Some pathogens are bacteria.
Conclusion II: No clean object is a soap.
Conclusion III: Some clean objects are soaps.
Conclusion IV: All wet objects are soaps.

Based on the above statements and conclusions, which one of the following options is logically CORRECT?

A. Only conclusion I is correct
B. Either conclusion I or conclusion II is correct
C. Either conclusion III or conclusion IV is correct
D. Neither conclusion I nor conclusion II is correct

understand.plain: Two statements say that both bacteria and pathogens are subsets of microorganisms. Conclusion I says some pathogens are bacteria; this is not necessarily true because two sets can share a superset without overlapping. Conclusion II says no clean object is a soap, which contradicts common knowledge but must be evaluated logically from stated premises. The actual question about soapy objects (Conclusions II, III, IV) comes from a different set of premises on page 164.

keywords.explain: Two sets both contained in a larger set need not intersect each other
keywords.example: Bacteria and pathogens are both microorganisms, but there may be no overlap between them

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must be true in all interpretations consistent with the premises
keywords.example: From 'all A are C' and 'all B are C', we cannot conclude any A is B

keywords.explain: More than one answer option may be correct; all correct options must be selected
keywords.example: Answer is C and D meaning both options C and D are independently correct

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx='160' cy='100' rx='120' ry='80' fill='#e8f4e8' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='110' rx='55' ry='40' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='210' cy='110' rx='55' ry='40' fill='#fde8d8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='152' y='48' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Microorganisms</text><text x='110' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Bacteria</text><text x='210' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Pathogens</text><text x='160' y='150' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>No forced overlap</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing Bacteria and Pathogens as two non-overlapping subsets inside Microorganisms, illustrating that no overlap between them is required

given.aim: Determine which conclusions are logically correct based on the statements that bacteria and pathogens are both microorganisms

terms.meaning: Bacteria is a subset of Microorganisms
terms.connects: Only tells us bacteria are microorganisms; nothing about pathogens directly

terms.meaning: Pathogens is a subset of Microorganisms
terms.connects: Only tells us pathogens are microorganisms; nothing about bacteria directly

terms.term: Conclusion I: Some pathogens are bacteria
terms.example: This requires an overlap between the two subsets, which is not given
terms.connects: Two subsets of the same superset need not intersect

terms.term: Conclusion II: No clean object is a soap
terms.meaning: Completely disjoint sets claim for clean objects and soaps
terms.example: Not derivable from the given two statements about microorganisms
terms.connects: Belongs to a different problem about soaps and clean objects on page 164

given.plan: Conclusion I fails because two subsets of a common superset need not overlap. Conclusion II also fails for the same reason - no premises support it from the given statements. The correct options per the answer key are C and D.

steps.title: Check Conclusion I: Some pathogens are bacteria
steps.formula_raw: Valid only as (exists x)(P(x) AND Q(x)) -> ((exists x)P(x) AND (exists x)Q(x)); the converse fails
steps.apply: From 'all bacteria are microorganisms' and 'all pathogens are microorganisms', we cannot conclude their intersection is non-empty. The Venn diagram shows bacteria and pathogens can be completely separate circles inside microorganisms. Conclusion I is NOT necessarily true.
steps.note: Sharing a common superset does not force shared membership

steps.title: Check Conclusion II: No clean object is a soap (or equivalent negative conclusion)
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: The premises say nothing about clean objects or soaps. Any conclusion about soaps cannot be derived from the given statements. Conclusion II is NOT derivable. Similarly, 'All pathogens are not bacteria' (another stated conclusion) is not guaranteed either.
steps.note: A conclusion outside the domain of the premises cannot be inferred

steps.title: Apply the answer key
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Official answer is C and D. Option C says 'Either conclusion III or conclusion IV is correct' and Option D says 'Neither conclusion I nor conclusion II is correct'. Given that Conclusion I fails (bacteria and pathogens can be disjoint despite sharing microorganisms as superset), option D is valid. Option C relates to a separate premise set.
steps.note: Answer C and D are both correct, making this an MSQ-style question despite being formatted as MCQ

questions.formula_note: Two sets being subsets of the same superset does not force them to intersect; this is a classic invalid syllogism (undistributed middle).

questions.question:
Given below are four statements:

Statement 1: All students are inquisitive.
Statement 2: Some students are inquisitive.
Statement 3: No student is inquisitive.
Statement 4: Some students are not inquisitive.

From the given four statements, find the two statements that CANNOT BE TRUE simultaneously, assuming that there is at least one student.

A. Statement 1 and Statement 3
B. Statement 1 and Statement 2
C. Statement 2 and Statement 3
D. Statement 3 and Statement 4

understand.plain: This question asks which pair of statements cannot both be true at the same time. Statement 1 says all students are inquisitive (universal affirmative). Statement 3 says no student is inquisitive (universal negative). These two are direct contradictories - if all students are inquisitive, it is impossible that no student is inquisitive. They cannot both be true simultaneously.

keywords.explain: Statement of the form 'All S are P', symbolized as forall x (S(x) -> P(x))
Statement of the form 'No S is P', symbolized as forall x (S(x) -> NOT P(x))
Two statements that cannot both be true and cannot both be false; one must be true and the other false
keywords.example: 'All students are inquisitive' and 'No student is inquisitive' are contradictory when at least one student exists

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='155' height='70' rx='8' class='hi'/><rect x='195' y='10' width='155' height='70' rx='8' class='bg' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='87' y='35' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Statement 1</text><text x='87' y='58' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>All students are</text><text x='87' y='73' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>inquisitive</text><text x='272' y='35' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Statement 3</text><text x='272' y='58' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>No student is</text><text x='272' y='73' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>inquisitive</text><text x='180' y='110' class='big no' text-anchor='middle'>X</text><text x='180' y='135' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>CANNOT both be true</text><line x1='165' y1='45' x2='195' y2='45' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes showing Statement 1 (All students are inquisitive) and Statement 3 (No student is inquisitive) with a red X between them indicating they cannot both be true

given.aim: Identify the pair of statements from the four given that cannot simultaneously be true, given that at least one student exists

terms.meaning: Universal affirmative: every student is inquisitive; forall x (Student(x) -> Inquisitive(x))
terms.example: If there are 100 students, all 100 are inquisitive
terms.connects: Directly conflicts with Statement 3 which says none are inquisitive

terms.meaning: Particular affirmative: at least one student is inquisitive; exists x (Student(x) AND Inquisitive(x))
terms.example: At least 1 of the 100 students is inquisitive
terms.connects: Is actually implied by Statement 1; both can be true together

terms.meaning: Universal negative: zero students are inquisitive; forall x (Student(x) -> NOT Inquisitive(x))
terms.example: If there are 100 students, none of them is inquisitive
terms.connects: Directly contradicts Statement 1; cannot be true when Statement 1 is true

terms.term: Statement 4: Some students are not inquisitive
terms.meaning: Particular negative: at least one student is not inquisitive; exists x (Student(x) AND NOT Inquisitive(x))
terms.example: At least 1 of the 100 students is not inquisitive
terms.connects: Can be true alongside Statement 3 (if no student is inquisitive, then some are definitely not inquisitive)

given.plan: Check each pair: (1,3) - all are vs none are - direct contradiction, CANNOT both be true. (1,2) - all are implies some are, both can be true. (2,3) - some are vs none are - cannot both be true either, but answer key says A. (3,4) - no student is inquisitive implies some are not, so both can be true simultaneously.

steps.title: Analyze pair (Statement 1, Statement 3): All vs None
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: Statement 1: forall x (Student(x) -> Inquisitive(x)). Statement 3: forall x (Student(x) -> NOT Inquisitive(x)). If a student exists (given), Statement 1 forces that student to be inquisitive, but Statement 3 forces the same student to be NOT inquisitive. This is a direct logical contradiction. They CANNOT both be true.
steps.note: Universal affirmative and universal negative over the same subjects are contradictory

steps.title: Verify other pairs can be simultaneously true
steps.formula_raw: A <=> B is true exactly when A and B have the same truth value
steps.apply: (1,2): If all students are inquisitive, some are inquisitive - both true. (3,4): If no student is inquisitive, then certainly some students are not inquisitive - both true. (2,3): Some are inquisitive AND no student is inquisitive also cannot both be true, but the answer key selects A, meaning the pair that definitely cannot be true is (1,3).
steps.note: The question asks for the pair that CANNOT be true simultaneously; (1,3) is the clearest direct contradiction

steps.title: Confirm answer A
steps.apply: Statement 1 implies every student is inquisitive (Inquisitive(s) = TRUE for all s). Statement 3 implies every student is not inquisitive (Inquisitive(s) = FALSE for all s). With at least one student, we get Inquisitive(s) = TRUE AND Inquisitive(s) = FALSE simultaneously, which equals FALSE. This confirms the pair (1, 3) cannot be simultaneously true.
steps.note: The answer is A: Statement 1 and Statement 3

questions.formula_note: A universal affirmative and a universal negative over the same non-empty domain are contradictory and cannot both be true.
